title: Topic 494: The Psychology of Auto Battlers: Hands-Off Gaming
content:
# Topic 494: The Psychology of Auto Battlers: Hands-Off Gaming

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the fascinating world of auto battlers, a genre of video games where players assemble a team of heroes that then fight automatically. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this seemingly passive form of entertainment, examining how principles of operant conditioning, intermittent reinforcement, and cognitive biases conspire to create a uniquely addictive experience. Through a rigorous, yet humorous, analysis of popular titles such as *Teamfight Tactics* and *Hearthstone Battlegrounds*, we reveal how the illusion of control, the thrill of the unknown, and the satisfaction of a well-executed strategy combine to make auto battlers a compelling and surprisingly deep form of entertainment. This paper will argue that, far from being a “hands-off” experience, auto battlers are a masterclass in psychological manipulation, a digital Skinner box that we willingly, and often gleefully, step into.

## INTRODUCTION

In the ever-expanding universe of video games, a new star has risen, a genre that defies traditional notions of gameplay and player agency. It is a world where the player is both a master strategist and a helpless spectator, a god-like figure who assembles an army and then watches from the sidelines as they charge into battle. This is the world of auto battlers, a genre that has taken the gaming world by storm, and the subject of our investigation.

But what is it about these games that has captured the hearts and minds of millions? What is the secret sauce that makes them so… sticky? Is it the thrill of victory, the agony of defeat, or the simple, primal pleasure of watching little digital men beat each other up? Or is there something deeper at play, a subtle and insidious manipulation of our very psychology?

This paper will argue for the latter. We will explore the psychological principles that make auto battlers so compelling, from the intermittent reinforcement schedules that keep us coming back for more, to the cognitive biases that make us believe we are in control, even when we are at the mercy of the random number generator. We will delve into the dark arts of game design, and reveal how developers use these principles to create a uniquely addictive experience.

So, sit back, relax, and prepare to have your mind blown. Or, at the very least, to learn a little something about why you just can’t seem to put down that game of *Teamfight Tactics*. And who knows, you might even come away with a newfound appreciation for the subtle genius of the auto battler, a genre that has perfected the art of making us feel like we are in control, even when we are just along for the ride.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has been somewhat slow to catch on to the auto battler phenomenon. While there is a wealth of research on video games in general, and even on specific genres like MOBAs and MMORPGs, the humble auto battler has been largely ignored. This is, perhaps, understandable. After all, how can a game where you don't directly control the action be worthy of serious academic inquiry? It's like studying the psychological impact of watching paint dry, but with more dragons and explosions.

However, what the literature lacks in specificity, it more than makes up for in general principles. The psychological hooks that make auto battlers so addictive are well-documented in other contexts. The work of B.F. Skinner on operant conditioning, for example, is essential reading for anyone who wants to understand why we keep coming back to these games, even when we know we should be doing something more productive, like our actual jobs. Skinner's experiments with pigeons, in which he trained them to peck at a button to receive a food pellet, are a chillingly accurate metaphor for the auto battler experience. We are the pigeons, the game is the Skinner box, and the rare, glorious victory is our food pellet.

More recent research has built on Skinner's work, exploring the power of intermittent reinforcement schedules. As Chou (2023) notes, games that use a variable ratio reinforcement schedule, where the reward is delivered after an unpredictable number of responses, are the most addictive. This is the same principle that makes slot machines so compelling, and it is at the heart of the auto battler experience. The random nature of the shop, the unpredictable outcomes of the battles, the tantalizing possibility of getting that one perfect unit to complete your build – it's all a form of intermittent reinforcement, a carefully crafted system designed to keep us hooked.

Of course, it's not all about operant conditioning. There are other psychological factors at play as well. The concept of cognitive biases, for example, is crucial to understanding the auto battler phenomenon. The illusion of control, the gambler's fallacy, the confirmation bias – these are all at play when we sit down to a game of *Teamfight Tactics* or *Hearthstone Battlegrounds*. We believe we are making strategic decisions, that our skill and ingenuity will lead us to victory. And to some extent, we are. But we are also at the mercy of the random number generator, a fact that we often choose to ignore, especially when we are on a winning streak.

In short, while the academic literature may not have a specific section on auto battlers, it provides us with all the tools we need to understand them. By drawing on the work of Skinner, Chou, and others, we can begin to piece together a psychological profile of the auto battler player, a person who is both a master strategist and a willing participant in their own conditioning.

## ANALYSIS

Now that we have a firm grasp of the psychological principles at play, let's take a closer look at how they are implemented in the auto battler genre. At its core, the auto battler is a masterclass in the application of operant conditioning and intermittent reinforcement. The entire game loop is designed to keep the player engaged, to keep them coming back for more, even when the odds are stacked against them.

The shopping phase is a perfect example of this. The player is presented with a random selection of units, and they must use their limited resources to assemble the best possible team. This is a classic variable ratio reinforcement schedule. The player never knows when they will be presented with the perfect unit, the one that will complete their build and lead them to victory. This uncertainty is what makes the shopping phase so exciting, and so addictive. It's the same feeling that a gambler gets when they pull the lever on a slot machine, the thrill of the unknown, the tantalizing possibility of a big win.

The battle phase, on the other hand, is a more subtle form of reinforcement. The player has no direct control over the action, but they are still invested in the outcome. A win is a form of positive reinforcement, a reward for making good decisions in the shopping phase. A loss, on the other hand, is a form of negative punishment, a consequence for making poor decisions. This feedback loop is what helps the player to learn and improve, to refine their strategies and to become a better player.

But it's not just about reinforcement. Auto battlers also tap into a number of cognitive biases to create a more compelling experience. The illusion of control is perhaps the most important of these. The player is given a great deal of agency in the shopping phase, and this creates the illusion that they are in control of their own destiny. They believe that their decisions are the primary factor in determining the outcome of the game, even when they are at the mercy of the random number generator. This illusion of control is what makes the game so satisfying when you win, and so frustrating when you lose.

The gambler's fallacy is another cognitive bias that is at play in auto battlers. This is the belief that if something happens more frequently than normal during a given period, it will happen less frequently in the future. In the context of auto battlers, this can manifest as the belief that if you have been unlucky with your shop rolls, you are “due” for a good roll. This is, of course, a fallacy. The odds of getting a particular unit are the same on every roll, regardless of what has happened in the past. But this belief can be a powerful motivator, and it can keep players in the game long after they should have given up.

Finally, there is the confirmation bias, the tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information in a way that confirms or supports one's prior beliefs or values. In auto battlers, this can manifest as the tendency to overvalue certain units or strategies, simply because they have worked for you in the past. This can lead to a rigid and inflexible playstyle, and it can prevent you from adapting to the ever-changing meta of the game.

In conclusion, the auto battler is a complex and sophisticated machine, a carefully crafted system of psychological manipulation that is designed to keep us hooked. By understanding the principles of operant conditioning, intermittent reinforcement, and cognitive biases, we can begin to see the auto battler for what it truly is: a digital Skinner box that we willingly, and often gleefully, step into.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly understand the psychological power of auto battlers, we must look beyond the realm of theory and into the real world. The stories of players who have been captivated by these games are a testament to their addictive nature, and they provide a rich source of data for our analysis.

Take, for example, the case of “Timmy,” a 25-year-old office worker who found himself spending upwards of 40 hours a week playing *Teamfight Tactics*. Timmy was not a hardcore gamer by any stretch of the imagination. He had a full-time job, a girlfriend, and a social life. But there was something about *Teamfight Tactics* that he just couldn’t resist. He would play during his lunch break, on the bus ride home, and late into the night. He would even sneak in a few games at his desk when his boss wasn’t looking.

Timmy’s story is not unique. There are countless others like him, people who have found themselves drawn into the world of auto battlers, often to the detriment of their other responsibilities. The forums and subreddits dedicated to these games are filled with stories of players who have neglected their work, their studies, and even their relationships in their pursuit of victory.

But it’s not all doom and gloom. For every story of addiction and neglect, there is a story of community and connection. The auto battler community is a vibrant and passionate one, and it is filled with players who are always willing to help each other out. They share strategies, they discuss the meta, and they celebrate each other’s victories. For many players, the community is just as important as the game itself.

One of the most fascinating aspects of the auto battler community is the way in which it has developed its own language and culture. Terms like “high-rolling,” “donkey-rolling,” and “forcing a comp” are all part of the auto battler lexicon, and they are used to describe specific strategies and playstyles. This shared language creates a sense of belonging, and it helps to foster a sense of community among players.

The rise of streaming and esports has also had a major impact on the auto battler community. Professional players like “Kripparrian” and “Disguised Toast” have become celebrities in their own right, and their streams are watched by thousands of viewers every day. These players are not just entertainers, they are also educators, and they have helped to popularize the genre and to teach new players the ropes.

In conclusion, the real-world examples of auto battler players and communities provide a rich and complex picture of the genre. They show us that these games are not just simple Skinner boxes, but are also complex social spaces where players can connect with each other, learn from each other, and share their passion for the game. And while the addictive nature of these games is a cause for concern, it is also a testament to their power and their appeal.

## DISCUSSION

What, then, are the broader implications of the auto battler phenomenon? What can these seemingly simple games teach us about human behavior, consumer habits, and the future of entertainment? The answers, we believe, are both profound and a little bit terrifying.

First and foremost, the success of auto battlers is a powerful reminder of the enduring power of operant conditioning. In an age of increasingly complex and sophisticated video games, the auto battler has shown us that the simple, primal pleasure of a well-timed reward is still a potent motivator. This has significant implications for the gaming industry, and for any industry that relies on user engagement. The principles of intermittent reinforcement and variable ratio rewards are not just for game designers. They can be applied to any product or service, from social media platforms to online shopping sites. The auto battler is a case study in how to keep users coming back for more, and it is a lesson that we would all do well to learn.

Secondly, the auto battler phenomenon sheds light on the complex relationship between skill and chance in our lives. We like to believe that we are the masters of our own destiny, that our successes are the result of our own hard work and ingenuity. But the auto battler reminds us that we are also at the mercy of forces beyond our control. The random number generator is a metaphor for the unpredictable nature of life itself, and the auto battler is a safe and controlled environment in which we can grapple with this uncertainty. This may be one of the reasons why these games are so compelling. They allow us to experience the thrill of the unknown, without any of the real-world consequences.

Finally, the auto battler phenomenon has implications for our understanding of consumer behavior. The auto battler player is a unique type of consumer, one who is willing to invest a great deal of time and energy into a product that offers no tangible rewards. They are not playing for money, or for fame, or for any of the other traditional motivators. They are playing for the simple, intrinsic pleasure of the game itself. This is a powerful reminder that not all consumer behavior is rational. We are not always motivated by a desire to maximize our own self-interest. Sometimes, we are just looking for a little bit of fun.

In conclusion, the auto battler is more than just a game. It is a mirror that reflects our own psychology, our own desires, and our own vulnerabilities. It is a testament to the power of operant conditioning, the allure of the unknown, and the simple, primal pleasure of a well-executed strategy. And as we move forward into an increasingly digital world, the lessons of the auto battler will only become more relevant.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand tapestry of human achievement, the auto battler may seem like a minor thread. It is, after all, just a game. But as we have seen, it is a game that is deeply rooted in the principles of human psychology, a game that has much to teach us about ourselves and about the world we live in.

We have explored the psychological hooks that make these games so addictive, from the intermittent reinforcement schedules that keep us coming back for more, to the cognitive biases that make us believe we are in control, even when we are at the mercy of the random number generator. We have seen how these principles are implemented in popular titles like *Teamfight Tactics* and *Hearthstone Battlegrounds*, and we have examined the real-world impact of these games on players and communities.

So, what is the final verdict on the auto battler? Is it a harmless diversion, a fun and engaging way to pass the time? Or is it a cynical and manipulative machine, a digital Skinner box that is designed to exploit our psychological vulnerabilities? The truth, as is so often the case, lies somewhere in between.

The auto battler is a powerful tool, and like any tool, it can be used for good or for ill. It can be a source of community and connection, a way for people to come together and to share their passion for a common interest. But it can also be a source of addiction and neglect, a time-sink that can consume our lives if we are not careful.

Ultimately, the responsibility lies with us, the players. We must be mindful of the psychological forces at play, and we must be willing to step away from the game when it is no longer serving us. The auto battler is a fun and engaging pastime, but it is not a substitute for a rich and fulfilling life. So, by all means, enjoy the game. But don’t forget to touch grass every once in a while.

[1] Chou, Y. (2023). *Gamification Meets Operant Conditioning in Game Design*. Retrieved from https://yukaichou.com/gamification-study/gamification-and-operant-conditioning/

[2] Skinner, B. F. (1953). *Science and human behavior*. Simon and Schuster.

[3] Weir, G. (2021, October 4). The rise of auto-battlers. *The Oxford Student*. Retrieved from https://www.oxfordstudent.com/2021/10/04/the-rise-of-auto-battlers/
